differential.evidence_basis: Acquired, sharply-demarcated, completely depigmented (chalk-white) macules in a symmetrical periorificial and acral distribution — the classic pattern of non-segmental vitiligo.

A common pigmentary disorder, but produces fine branny scaling and mild HYPOpigmentation (not stark chalk-white depigmentation) and favours the upper trunk rather than a periorificial/acral face-and-hands pattern.
Poorly-defined, mildly scaly, off-white patches, usually on the cheeks of children — the ill-defined borders and partial hypopigmentation contrast with the sharp, complete depigmentation here.

Can present with hypopigmented patches, but characteristically with LOSS of sensation, a thickened peripheral nerve, and an active raised border — must be actively excluded, particularly given migration/epidemiology, before it is dismissed.

The acute presenting DISTRESS (hyperventilation, tachycardia, catastrophic fear) is a panic attack secondary to the visible skin change — the emotional emergency, not the dermatological diagnosis, and the thing telehealth must de-escalate.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The early move is to recognise the classic depigmentation pattern as benign WHILE screening the two things that change management — the 'important not to miss' sensory-loss features of leprosy, and the patient's mental-state / self-harm risk — rather than being swept into an emergency escalation by the panic and the 'flesh-eating' fear.

differential.evidence_basis: Sharp complete depigmentation, symmetrical periorificial + acral distribution, leukotrichia within an affected area, NORMAL sensation in the patches, and no scaling or active border — the constellation is diagnostic of vitiligo and excludes the mimics.
Excluded by the complete (not partial) depigmentation, the total absence of fine scaling, and the periorificial/acral rather than truncal distribution.
Excluded by NORMAL sensation within the patches, no thickened peripheral nerve, and no active erythematous border.
The concurrent acute condition that actually needs same-encounter action — de-escalation and a self-harm-risk screen — even though the skin disease itself is benign and routine.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Once sensation is confirmed normal and no scaling/active border is present, vitiligo is secure and the mimics fall away. The remaining live task is managing the panic and safety-netting the psychological risk — NOT an urgent dermatological escalation.

differential.diagnosis: vitiligo (non-segmental), with an acute reactive panic attack
differential.evidence_basis: A benign, chronic autoimmune pigmentary condition presenting with an acute PSYCHOLOGICAL crisis. Physically harmless and routinely managed; the acute need is reassurance, de-escalation, sun protection and routine GP/dermatology referral, with a mental-health safety-net.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Final answer is benign vitiligo. The teeth of the case are (a) resisting over-triage of the skin condition despite the alarming framing, and (b) still delivering an explicit mental-health safety-net for the acute distress.

red_flags.description: Acute severe psychological distress with a possible risk of self-harm — thoughts of self-harm/suicide, or an inability to keep oneself safe, arising from the health-anxiety crisis
red_flags.clinical_significance: Acute health-anxiety crises carry a small but real self-harm risk; a sensitive direct screen is mandatory, and any positive response converts a routine dermatology encounter into a mental-health emergency.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Failing to screen and safety-net the psychological risk could leave an acutely distressed patient without a crisis pathway — the most dangerous omission in this otherwise benign case.

red_flags.description: Sensory loss within the patches, a thickened peripheral nerve, or an active raised erythematous border — features of tuberculoid leprosy rather than vitiligo
red_flags.clinical_significance: Distinguishes an important, treatable infectious mimic from benign vitiligo; normal sensation and no active border exclude it here.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Mislabelling a hypopigmented, anaesthetic patch as vitiligo would miss leprosy and delay curative multidrug therapy and contact tracing.

red_flags.description: Cardiac or respiratory cause masquerading as the 'panic' — true chest pain, syncope, or breathlessness not explained by hyperventilation
red_flags.clinical_significance: Panic-attack symptoms overlap with cardiac/respiratory emergencies; chest pain or syncope would require urgent assessment rather than reassurance.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Anchoring on 'just a panic attack' could miss a genuine cardiorespiratory event; here chest pain is denied and speech is unimpaired.

red_flags.description: Atypical or changing skin features — a new scaling, itching, blistering, ulcerating, bleeding, or rapidly enlarging pigmented lesion within or near the patches
red_flags.clinical_significance: Would point away from benign depigmentation toward an inflammatory, infective or neoplastic process needing in-person examination.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Dismissing a genuinely evolving lesion as vitiligo could delay diagnosis of an alternative dermatosis or malignancy.

pathophysiology_summary: Vitiligo is an acquired autoimmune depigmenting disorder in which cytotoxic (CD8+) T-cell mediated destruction of epidermal melanocytes causes discrete, complete loss of pigment. The result is sharply-demarcated, chalk-white (fully depigmented) macules, characteristically symmetrical and favouring periorificial (mouth, eyes) and acral (hands) sites; involvement of hair follicles produces leukotrichia (white hairs) within affected areas. Because only pigment cells are lost, the skin is otherwise structurally and functionally normal — there is no scaling, induration, sensory loss, or textural change, which distinguishes it from tinea versicolor (superficial fungal scaling, partial hypopigmentation), pityriasis alba (ill-defined mild scaly hypopigmentation) and tuberculoid leprosy (hypopigmentation WITH anaesthesia and nerve involvement). Progression is unpredictable and can be episodic, often flaring after physical or psychological stress. It carries an increased association with other autoimmune conditions, particularly autoimmune thyroid disease, justifying a baseline autoimmune/thyroid screen at GP follow-up. The condition is physically benign; its major morbidity is psychosocial, which in this presentation has precipitated an acute panic attack — a reactive hyperventilation-driven surge of sympathetic symptoms (tachycardia, tachypnoea) that resolves with reassurance and controlled breathing and is not itself a medical emergency in the absence of cardiorespiratory red flags.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Escalating a benign, chronic pigmentary condition to urgent same-day in-person review, an emergency department or an ambulance because of the patient's terror, the 'flesh-eating' language and the source's urgency framing. Over-triage of the SKIN condition is the primary scored defect.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Treating the feared infection instead of the anxiety
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Reaching for antifungals or antibiotics to treat a feared infectious/'flesh-eating' process — an error of commission. There is no infection; the acute therapeutic target is the panic (reassurance + breathing) and the chronic one is repigmentation via routine specialist care.
Failing to check sensation and the lesion border, and so not actively excluding tuberculoid leprosy — the one important-not-to-miss mimic. Normal sensation and no active border exclude it here.

Over-correcting toward dermatological reassurance and forgetting to screen for self-harm and provide an explicit mental-health crisis safety-net for the acute severe distress — the critical under-triage for this case.
Attributing all the acute symptoms to anxiety without a brief screen for chest pain, syncope or breathlessness that could signify a cardiorespiratory emergency.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Clinician palpation of skin texture / induration within the patches
Formal sensory testing (light touch, temperature, pinprick) to exclude leprosy
Wood's-lamp examination to accentuate the depigmentation
Measured vital signs (BP, heart rate, respiratory rate, SpO2)
A skin scraping / KOH microscopy to exclude a superficial fungal cause
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Recognise benign vitiligo from the video-observable pattern and patient-reported normal sensation; actively de-escalate the panic in-session with calm reassurance and slow controlled (box) breathing; explicitly screen for self-harm risk and provide a mental-health crisis safety-net; explain the condition is not an infection, cancer or 'flesh-eating' disease and is physically harmless; advise strict daily SPF 50+ sun protection to the depigmented areas; and arrange ROUTINE GP review (1–2 weeks) for a baseline autoimmune/thyroid screen, psychological support and dermatology referral for repigmentation options. Emergency escalation is reserved for the conditional mental-health or cardiorespiratory red flags, not the skin condition.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Low for the dermatological diagnosis — the pattern (sharp complete depigmentation, symmetrical periorificial/acral distribution, leukotrichia, normal sensation, no scaling) is classic and largely video-observable. The real risks are (1) OVER-action on the skin condition driven by the patient's panic and the 'flesh-eating' framing, and (2) UNDER-action on the psychological crisis (no self-harm screen, no mental-health safety-net). The correct answer resists the first and delivers the second.
